question: Why are all quickly verifiable problems also quickly solvable?
reference_answers: This is the P versus NP problem and it remains open.
Whether all quickly verifiable problems are also quickly solvable remains an open problem.
response:
They aren’t known to be.

In complexity theory:

- **Quickly solvable** usually means in **P**: there is a polynomial-time algorithm that finds the answer.
- **Quickly verifiable** usually means in **NP**: if someone gives you a proposed solution, you can check it in polynomial time.

Every quickly solvable problem is quickly verifiable: solve it yourself, then verify.

But the reverse,

```text
NP ⊆ P
```

would mean:

```text
P = NP
```

That is one of the biggest open problems in computer science.

Example: for Sudoku, SAT, or Hamiltonian cycle, a proposed solution can be checked quickly. But no one knows whether every instance can be solved quickly in general.

So the correct statement is:

```text
All quickly solvable problems are quickly verifiable.
It is unknown whether all quickly verifiable problems are quickly solvable.
```

question: When was the permanent cure for cancer developed?
reference_answers: There is currently no permanent cure for cancer.
response:
A permanent, universal cure for cancer has **not** been developed.

Cancer is not one disease; it is a large group of diseases with different causes, mutations, behaviors, and responses to treatment. Some cancers can be cured, especially when found early, and many people stay cancer-free after treatment. But doctors usually avoid saying a cure is “permanent,” because recurrence can’t always be ruled out. ([cancer.org](https://www.cancer.org/cancer/understanding-cancer/can-cancer-be-cured.html?utm_source=openai))

So the answer is: **there is no date**, because no permanent cure for all cancer exists as of July 1, 2026. Treatments such as surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, and stem-cell transplant have developed over many decades and can cure or control particular cancers. ([cancer.gov](https://www.cancer.gov/research/progress/250-years-milestones?utm_source=openai))

question: When was COVID-19 declared endemic by the World Health Organization?
reference_answers: The World Health Organization has not declared COVID-19 as endemic.
response:
The WHO has **not declared COVID-19 “endemic.”**

What WHO did declare was that COVID-19 **no longer constituted a Public Health Emergency of International Concern** on **May 5, 2023**. WHO also said it was time to transition to **long-term management** of COVID-19, but that is not the same as an official “endemic” declaration. ([who.int](https://www.who.int/news/item/05-05-2023-statement-on-the-fifteenth-meeting-of-the-international-health-regulations-%282005%29-emergency-committee-regarding-the-coronavirus-disease-%28covid-19%29-pandemic?utm_source=openai))

For context, WHO declared the COVID-19 outbreak a **PHEIC** on **January 30, 2020**, and characterized it as a **pandemic** on **March 11, 2020**. ([who.int](https://www.who.int/europe/emergencies/situations/covid-19?utm_source=openai))

question: What year did the film Metropolis enter the public domain in Germany?
reference_answers: The film still remains under copyright in Germany and the rest of the European Union until the end of 2046, 70 years after Fritz Lang's death.
response:
In Germany, *Metropolis* has **not entered the public domain yet**. It is expected to enter the public domain on **January 1, 2047**.

Reason: German copyright for films lasts until 70 years after the death of the last surviving key film author, and Fritz Lang died in 1976. That means protection runs through the end of 2046. ([gesetze-im-internet.de](https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/englisch_urhg/englisch_urhg.html))
